questions.question: According to Section 5.1.2, what is the role of PSA testing in the pathway to prostate cancer diagnosis?

options.text: A PSA test is only required for patients following the organised screening pathway
A PSA test will be part of the diagnosis regardless of which pathway a patient goes through
A PSA test is reserved for cases where digital rectal examination is abnormal
A PSA test is only used when biopsy is being considered

questions.explanation: Section 5.1.2 states that 'Regardless of which pathway a patient goes through to his PCa diagnosis, a PSA test will be part of it.' This indicates that PSA testing is universal across all diagnostic pathways for prostate cancer.
questions.fact: Regardless of which pathway a patient goes through to his PCa diagnosis, a PSA test will be part of it.

questions.question: At what age is PSA testing recommended to begin for men with no other risk factors?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.4 states that 'early testing with PSA can be recommended starting at the age of 50' for men with no other risk factors. Age 45 applies to men with family history or African descent, and age 40 applies to BRCA2 carriers.
questions.fact: Early testing with PSA can be recommended starting at the age of 50 for men with no other risk factors.

questions.question: What PSA level at age 60 defines a patient requiring two-year follow-up intervals?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.4 specifies that 'follow-up intervals of two years may be offered to those initially at risk (PSA > 1 ng/mL at 40 years; PSA > 2 ng/mL at 60 years).' This distinguishes the threshold between different age groups.
questions.fact: Follow-up intervals of two years may be offered to those initially at risk (PSA > 1 ng/mL at 40 years; PSA > 2 ng/mL at 60 years).

questions.question: According to the PIVOT and ERSPC trial data, asymptomatic men with a life expectancy of less than how many years are unlikely to benefit from early prostate cancer diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.4 explicitly states 'Asymptomatic men who have less than a 15-year life expectancy are unlikely to benefit from an early diagnosis of prostate cancer, based on data from the Prostate Cancer Intervention Versus Observation Trial (PIVOT) and the European Randomised Screening for Prostate Cancer (ERSPC) trials.'
questions.fact: Asymptomatic men who have less than a 15-year life expectancy are unlikely to benefit from an early diagnosis of prostate cancer, based on data from the PIVOT and ERSPC trials.

questions.question: What is the primary aim of the diagnostic pathway for prostate cancer (PCa)?

options.text: Detect all cases of prostate cancer regardless of significance
Achieve detection of significant PCa while leaving insignificant PCa undetected
Maximize the number of biopsies performed for diagnostic confirmation
Reduce the overall cost of healthcare delivery for urological conditions

questions.explanation: Section 5.1.1 explicitly states that the diagnostic pathway for PCa 'aims to achieve detection of significant PCa, while leaving insignificant PCa undetected.' This reflects the goal of balancing detection with avoiding overdiagnosis.
questions.fact: The diagnostic pathway for PCa aims to achieve detection of significant PCa, while leaving insignificant PCa undetected, balancing diagnostic accuracy with the burden on an individual and healthcare resources.

questions.question: According to the guideline, which of the following patient-specific factors should be considered in the diagnostic pathway for prostate cancer?

options.text: Lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), family history, age, and comorbidity
Serum testosterone levels, PSA density, and prostate volume only
Family history and ethnic background exclusively
Age, BMI, and cardiovascular risk factors primarily

questions.explanation: Section 5.1.1 specifically lists 'Patient-specific factors such as lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), family history, age and comorbidity' as factors that should always be considered in the diagnostic pathway.
questions.fact: Patient-specific factors such as lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), family history, age and comorbidity should always be considered.

questions.question: Through which of the following indications may men enter the diagnostic pathway for prostate cancer?

options.text: Clinical symptoms, opportunistic early detection (individual), or screening (population-based)
Only through opportunistic early detection recommended by the physician
Exclusively via clinical symptoms presented to a urologist

questions.explanation: Section 5.1.1 clearly states that 'Men may enter the diagnostic pathway through various indications, including clinical symptoms, opportunistic early detection (individual) or screening (population-based).' These represent the three main entry points.
questions.fact: Men may enter the diagnostic pathway through various indications, including clinical symptoms, opportunistic early detection (individual) or screening (population-based).

questions.question: Why does the prevalence of prostate cancer and significant prostate cancer vary in the diagnostic pathway?

options.text: Due to differences in laboratory equipment and PSA testing methods
Due to variations in biopsy techniques and pathologist expertise
Depending on the indication for testing, resulting in different yields of the diagnostic pathway
Because of differences in healthcare reimbursement policies across regions

questions.explanation: Section 5.1.1 explicitly states that 'The prevalence of PCa and significant PCa varies depending on the indication, resulting in different yields of the subsequent diagnostic pathway.' This acknowledges that clinical symptoms, individual early detection, and population screening have different pre-test probabilities.
questions.fact: The prevalence of PCa and significant PCa varies depending on the indication, resulting in different yields of the subsequent diagnostic pathway.

questions.question: In which clinical stage of prostate cancer are symptoms most likely to be absent?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'localised PCa is usually asymptomatic' while local progression and bone metastases cause symptoms. This is a direct recall of symptom presentation patterns in prostate cancer.
questions.fact: Symptoms usually occur late in the natural history of PCa, and localised PCa is therefore usually asymptomatic.

questions.question: Which of the following symptoms may occur with bone metastases from prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline text directly states that 'Bone metastases may cause pain or spinal cord compression.' This is an explicit statement about the clinical manifestations of skeletal involvement.
questions.fact: Bone metastases may cause pain or spinal cord compression.

questions.question: What is required for definitive diagnosis of prostate cancer according to the guideline?

options.text: Elevated serum PSA level above 10 ng/mL
Histopathological verification in prostate biopsy cores

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Definitive diagnosis normally depends on histopathological verification in prostate biopsy cores.' This directly answers the diagnostic requirement for PCa confirmation.
questions.fact: Definitive diagnosis normally depends on histopathological verification in prostate biopsy cores.

questions.question: Under what clinical scenario might men with high suspicion of prostate cancer avoid biopsy?

options.text: When PSA levels are between 4-10 ng/mL
When pre-existing comorbidities would exclude any treatment intensification
When the patient is under 60 years of age

questions.explanation: The guideline specifically states that men with high suspicion of malignancy (malignant feeling prostate, PSA >100ng/mL, positive bone scan) 'might avoid a biopsy, especially if pre-existing comorbidities would exclude any treatment intensification in the hormone-sensitive or castration-resistant phase.'
questions.fact: Men with high suspicion of malignancy (e.g. malignant feeling prostate, PSA >100ng/mL and a positive bone scan) might avoid a biopsy, especially if pre-existing comorbidities would exclude any treatment intensification.

questions.question: What is the recommended PSA threshold for biopsy in the IMPACT study screening program for men with BRCA1/2 mutations?
questions.explanation: According to section 5.1.6, the IMPACT study protocol recommends biopsy if PSA is greater than 3.0 ng/mL in men with germline BRCA1/2 mutations undergoing annual screening.
questions.section_title: 5.1.6. Genetic testing for inherited prostate cancer
questions.fact: biopsy recommended if PSA > 3.0ng/mL

questions.question: After three years of screening in the IMPACT study, which of the following was NOT observed in BRCA2 mutation carriers compared with noncarriers?
questions.explanation: Section 5.1.6 states that BRCA2 carriers showed higher PCa incidence, younger diagnosis age, and more clinically significant tumours. MSH2 findings were reported separately for the mismatch repair cohort, not as a finding in BRCA2 carriers.
questions.section_title: 5.1.6. Genetic testing for inherited prostate cancer
questions.fact: BRCA2 mutation carriers were associated with a higher incidence of PCa, a younger age of diagnosis, and more clinically significant tumours compared with noncarriers

questions.question: In the IMPACT study, what was the influence of BRCA1 mutations on prostate cancer according to the available evidence?

options.text: BRCA1 mutations were clearly associated with higher PCa incidence
BRCA1 mutations were associated with more aggressive tumours
The influence of BRCA1 mutations on PCa remained unclear
BRCA1 mutations were protective against PCa

questions.explanation: Section 5.1.6 explicitly states that the influence of BRCA1 mutations on prostate cancer remained unclear, with no differences in age or tumour characteristics detected between BRCA1 carriers and noncarriers.
questions.section_title: 5.1.6. Genetic testing for inherited prostate cancer
questions.fact: The influence of BRCA1 mutations on PCa remained unclear

questions.question: Which pathogenic variants were studied in the mismatch repair cohort of the IMPACT study?
questions.explanation: According to section 5.1.6, the mismatch repair cohort of IMPACT evaluated men with MSH2 and MSH6 pathogenic variants, finding a higher incidence of significant prostate cancer versus noncarriers.
questions.section_title: 5.1.6. Genetic testing for inherited prostate cancer
questions.fact: The mismatch repair cohort of IMPACT in men with MSH2 and MSH6 pathogenic variants found a higher incidence of significant PCa versus noncarriers

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of patients with a PSA density below 0.09 ng/mL/cc were unlikely to be diagnosed with clinically significant prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4 states that patients with a PSA-D below 0.09 ng/mL/cc were found unlikely (4%) to be diagnosed with csPCa, making this the correct answer.
questions.fact: Patients with a PSA-D below 0.09 ng/mL/cc were found unlikely (4%) to be diagnosed with csPCa.

questions.question: In a series of 640 men, how much smaller were prostate volumes measured by TRUS compared to MRI?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4 states that in a series of 640 men, TRUS found prostate volumes on average 8% smaller than MRI, which is the correct answer based on the guideline text.
questions.fact: In a series of 640 men, TRUS found prostate volumes on average 8% smaller than MRI.

questions.question: Which imaging modality demonstrated better correlation with surgical specimen volume in 109 men who underwent radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4 clearly states that in the 109 men who underwent RP, MRI-derived prostate volume was better correlated to the volume of the surgical specimen than TRUS-derived volume.
questions.fact: In the 109 men who underwent RP, MRI-derived prostate volume was better correlated to the volume of the surgical specimen than TRUS-derived volume.

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what percentage of prostate cancer cases are detected by a suspect digital rectal examination (DRE) alone, irrespective of PSA level?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that in approximately 18% of cases, prostate cancer is detected by suspect DRE alone, irrespective of PSA level. This highlights the importance of DRE in prostate cancer detection even when PSA levels are normal.
questions.fact: In approximately 18% of cases, PCa is detected by suspect DRE alone, irrespective of PSA level [187].

questions.question: What is the positive predictive value (PPV) of a suspect DRE in patients with a PSA level ≤ 4ng/mL?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that a suspect DRE in patients with a PSA level ≤ 4ng/mL has a positive predictive value of 5-30%, indicating that DRE remains clinically relevant even in the 'normal' PSA range.
questions.fact: A suspect DRE in patients with a PSA level ≤ 4ng/mL has a positive predictive value (PPV) of 5-30% [187].

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what is the primary purpose of the PI-QUAL score in prostate MRI?

options.text: To assess image quality and decide when to repeat the examination
To predict prostate cancer aggressiveness and staging
To monitor response to androgen deprivation therapy
To estimate tumor volume for treatment planning

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4.c states that the PI-QUAL score provides simple criteria to assess image quality and decides when to repeat the examination, serving as a convenient tool to follow improvements in quality.
questions.fact: The prostate image quality (PI-QUAL) score provides simple criteria to assess image quality and decides when to repeat the examination [238] and is a convenient tool to follow improvements in quality [239].

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, what factor(s) primarily account for discrepancies between MRI and biopsy results?

options.text: Suboptimal image quality and basic mistakes in interpretation
Patient-related factors such as body mass index
Limitations of the ultrasound-guided biopsy technique

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4.c explicitly states that most discrepancies between MRI and biopsy results may be explained by suboptimal image quality and basic mistakes in interpretation, highlighting the importance of quality assurance programs.
questions.fact: Most discrepancies between MRI and biopsy results may be explained by suboptimal image quality and basic mistakes in interpretation [236].

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines, MRI performance has been shown to be better at which type of facilities and with which specialists?

options.text: Ambulatory imaging centres with rotating staff
Academic research centres with research fellows

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.4.c states that MRI performance is better with experienced radiologists and at high-volume centres, which currently limits its broad use by non-dedicated radiologists.
questions.fact: MRI performance is better with experienced radiologists and at high-volume centres, which currently limits its broad use by non-dedicated radiologists [237,240].

questions.question: What is considered the main limitation of PI-RADS criteria for prostate MRI interpretation?

options.text: They are too expensive to implement
They cannot detect clinically significant cancer

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.5 explicitly states that 'As the PI-RADS criteria remain subjective, approaches using quantitative thresholds for imaging biomarkers have been proposed.' This subjectivity is the primary driver for developing alternative quantitative methods.
questions.fact: The PI-RADS criteria remain subjective

questions.question: According to the guideline, what happens to AI algorithm performance when tested on images from a different vendor compared to their training dataset?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.5 states that 'AI algorithms tend to show decreased performance when tested on images from a different vendor or acquired with different image parameters as compared to their training dataset.' This is why testing on external cohorts is essential.
questions.fact: AI algorithms tend to show decreased performance when tested on images from a different vendor or acquired with different image parameters as compared to their training dataset

questions.question: In the prospective study comparing AI to human reading, what was the trade-off observed with the AI algorithm that detected csPCa missed by human reading?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.5 reports that in one study 'the algorithm detected csPCa missed by human reading in 4% of patients, but at the expense of a doubling of the false positive rate (0.66 vs. 1.39).' This illustrates the trade-off between improved detection and increased false positives.
questions.fact: The algorithm detected csPCa missed by human reading in 4% of patients, but at the expense of a doubling of the false positive rate (0.66 vs. 1.39)

questions.question: According to the EAU guidelines on micro-ultrasound, what is the reliability of standard transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) for detecting prostate cancer?

options.text: Not reliable for detecting prostate cancer
Reliable only when combined with MRI

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.5.b states that 'Standard TRUS is not reliable for detecting PCa [263]'. This is a direct statement indicating the limitation of standard TRUS in prostate cancer detection.
questions.fact: Standard TRUS is not reliable for detecting PCa

questions.question: What is the diagnostic yield of additional biopsies performed on hypoechoic lesions identified during TRUS, according to the EAU guidelines?

options.text: Substantial when combined with MRI-targeted biopsies

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.5.b explicitly states that 'the diagnostic yield of additional biopsies performed on hypoechoic lesions is negligible [264]'. This indicates that targeting hypoechoic lesions seen on TRUS provides minimal additional diagnostic value.
questions.fact: The diagnostic yield of additional biopsies performed on hypoechoic lesions is negligible

questions.question: What is the current status of artificial intelligence algorithms trained to detect prostate cancer on TRUS images, as described in the EAU guidelines?

options.text: Validated and recommended for routine clinical use
Showed promising preliminary results but require validation in independent cohorts
Proven to be inferior to standard TRUS

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.5.b states that 'Artificial intelligence algorithms trained to detect PCa on TRUS images have shown promising preliminary results, but confirmation of these results in independent cohorts is required'. This indicates that while initial data is encouraging, further validation is needed before clinical implementation.
questions.fact: Artificial intelligence algorithms trained to detect PCa on TRUS images have shown promising preliminary results, but confirmation of these results in independent cohorts is required

questions.question: Which of the following components were included in the multi-parametric ultrasound (US) used in the CADMUS trial?

options.text: B-mode, Colour Doppler, real-time elastography, and contrast-enhanced US
B-mode, spectral Doppler, shear-wave elastography, and contrast-enhanced US
Grey-scale, power Doppler, strain elastography, and harmonic imaging
B-mode, Colour Doppler, transrectal ultrasound, and contrast-enhanced US

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.6 explicitly states that in the CADMUS trial, multi-parametric US was composed of B-mode, Colour Doppler, real-time elastography, and contrast-enhanced US.
questions.section_title: 5.2.6. Prostate-specific membrane antigen-positron emission tomography/computed tomography
questions.fact: Multi-parametric US composed of B-mode, Colour Doppler, real-time elastography, and contrast-enhanced US

questions.question: How many patients underwent both multi-parametric MRI and multi-parametric US in the CADMUS trial?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.6 clearly states that in the CADMUS trial, 306 patients underwent both multi-parametric MRI and multi-parametric US.
questions.section_title: 5.2.6. Prostate-specific membrane antigen-positron emission tomography/computed tomography
questions.fact: 306 patients underwent both multi-parametric MRI and multi-parametric US

questions.question: According to the CADMUS trial results, compared to multi-parametric MRI, how much more additional patients did multi-parametric US submit to biopsy?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.6 explicitly states that multi-parametric US submitted 11.1% more patients to biopsy than MRI while detecting 4.3% fewer clinically significant prostate cancers.
questions.section_title: 5.2.6. Prostate-specific membrane antigen-positron emission tomography/computed tomography
questions.fact: Multi-parametric US submitted 11.1% more patients to biopsy than MRI

questions.question: What is the operating frequency and spatial resolution of high-resolution micro-ultrasonography (micro-US)?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that high-resolution micro-ultrasonography operates at 29 MHz with a spatial resolution of 70 microns, allowing visualization of normal prostate ducts and PCa foci that alter ductal architecture.
questions.fact: High-resolution micro-ultrasonography (micro-US) operates at 29 MHz with a spatial resolution of 70 microns.

questions.question: According to the PRI-MUS score for micro-ultrasonography focal lesions, which score range is considered suspicious for malignancy?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that the PRI-MUS score assessed malignancy risk with scores of 1-2 considered low-risk, 3 equivocal, and 4-5 suspicious for malignancy.
questions.fact: The Prostate Risk Identification using Micro-UltraSound (PRI-MUS) score has scores of 4-5 considered suspicious.

questions.question: In the first prospective trial comparing MRI and micro-US before combined targeted and systematic biopsy, what percentage of clinically significant prostate cancers (csPCa) were detected by micro-US-targeted biopsy?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that in the first prospective trial with 79 csPCa cases, MRI and micro-US detected 76% and 73% of csPCa respectively, while systematic sampling detected 57%.
questions.fact: MRI and micro-US detected 60 (76%) and 58 (73%) of the 79 csPCa, respectively.

questions.question: What limitation of micro-US was identified regarding visualization of the transition zone?

options.text: 23% of PI-RADS ≥ 3 lesions in the transition zone were invisible on micro-US
Transition zone lesions showed 100% visibility on micro-US
Micro-US had superior visualization of the transition zone compared to MRI
Only 5% of transition zone lesions were missed by micro-US

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that in a series of 92 PI-RADS ≥ 3 lesions in the transition zone, 21 (23%) were invisible on micro-US, even when the operator was aware of the MRI results.
questions.fact: In a series of 92 PI-RADS ≥ 3 lesions in the transition zone, 21 (23%) were invisible on micro-US.

questions.question: What was the reported interoperator variability (Light's kappa) for lesion localization with micro-US among six experienced readers?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that in a prospective series of 57 patients, the Light's kappa value for localizing lesions across six experienced readers was only 0.30 (95% CI: 0.21-0.39), indicating substantial interoperator variability.
questions.fact: Light's kappa value for localising the lesions across six experienced readers was only 0.30 (95% CI: 0.21-0.39).

questions.question: In a recent randomized controlled trial, what percentage of patients had negative findings with micro-US compared to MRI?
questions.explanation: The guideline reports that in a recent RCT, the percentage of patients with negative findings was 20% (71/347) for micro-US versus 32% (179/557) for MRI, indicating MRI had more negative findings.
questions.fact: In a recent RCT, the percentage of patients with negative findings was 20% (71/347) for micro-US versus 32% (179/557) for MRI.

questions.question: What is the current validation status of urine, serum, and tissue-based biomarkers for prostate cancer detection and risk stratification?

options.text: Their efficacy has not been validated
They have been validated for routine clinical use
They have been validated but require further testing
They have been validated only for high-risk patients

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.7.a states that 'their efficacy has not been validated [279]', referring to urine, serum, and tissue-based biomarkers proposed for improving prostate cancer detection and risk stratification.
questions.fact: their efficacy has not been validated [279]

questions.question: Which of the following types of biomarkers have been proposed for improving prostate cancer detection and risk stratification?
questions.explanation: According to Section 5.2.7.a, 'Urine and serum biomarkers, as well as tissue-based biomarkers, have been proposed' for improving detection and risk stratification of prostate cancer patients.
questions.fact: Urine and serum biomarkers, as well as tissue-based biomarkers, have been proposed for improving detection and risk stratification of PCa patients

questions.question: What potential clinical benefit do urine, serum, and tissue-based biomarkers offer in prostate cancer management, according to the guideline?

options.text: Potentially avoiding unnecessary biopsies and/or unnecessary imaging
Guaranteeing accurate diagnosis of all prostate cancers
Providing definitive treatment for prostate cancer

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.7.a notes that these biomarkers are proposed 'potentially avoiding unnecessary biopsies and/or unnecessary imaging', while also acknowledging their unvalidated efficacy.
questions.fact: potentially avoiding unnecessary biopsies and/or unnecessary imaging

questions.question: According to the guideline, what are the pooled sensitivity and specificity values reported for PSMA-PET imaging in detecting clinically significant prostate cancer (csPCa)?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.7 states that for csPCa detection, a pooled sensitivity of 0.89 and a pooled specificity of 0.56 have been reported, distinguishing these from the lower sensitivity and higher specificity values observed in the PRIMARY study.
questions.fact: For csPCa detection, a pooled sensitivity of 0.89 and a pooled specificity of 0.56 have been reported

questions.question: In the prospective trial of 291 patients comparing combined PSMA PET + MRI versus MRI alone, which parameter showed the LARGEST numerical improvement?
questions.explanation: The text states combined PSMA PET + MRI improved NPV from 72% to 91% (test ratio 1.27), representing a 19 percentage point improvement, which is larger than the sensitivity improvement of 14 percentage points.
questions.fact: Combined PSMA PET + MRI improved negative predictive value (NPV) compared with MRI alone (91% vs 72%, test ratio = 1.27 [1.11-1.39], p < 0.001)

questions.question: The PRIMARY score was shown to be valid only under which specific condition according to the guideline?

options.text: When using harmonised PET cameras and the same PSMA PET ligands
When combined with PI-RADS score 3 or higher
When used in patients with PSA levels above 10 ng/mL
When performed after at least 6 weeks of androgen deprivation therapy

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.7 explicitly states that 'The PRIMARY score was shown to be valid only when using harmonised PET cameras and the same PSMA PET ligands,' indicating strict technical requirements for its validity.
questions.fact: The PRIMARY score was shown to be valid only when using harmonised PET cameras and the same PSMA PET ligands

questions.question: According to the systematic review and meta-analysis cited in the guideline, what is the sensitivity of PSMA PET for csPCa?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that the systematic review revealed sensitivity of 82% (95% CI: 73-90%) for PSMA PET for csPCa, distinguishing this from the pooled analysis sensitivity of 89% and the prospective trial sensitivity of 97%.
questions.fact: A systematic review and meta-analysis revealed sensitivity of PSMA PET for csPCa of 82% (95% CI: 73-90%)

questions.question: The SPARC project offers adoption of frameworks for reporting of PSMA PET/CT. Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a component of this framework?
questions.explanation: Section 5.2.7 lists SPARC framework components including PRIMARY score, PSMA volume, SUV mean, SUV max, and miTNM PROMISE classification. Gleason grade group classification is not mentioned as part of the SPARC project.
questions.fact: The SPARC project offers the adoption of harmonised and reproducible frameworks for reporting of PSMA PET/CT, including molecular imaging (mi) for PSMA expression, miTNM PROMISE classification for reporting of PSMA PET/CT, the PRIMARY score for intraprostatic staging, PSMA volume, mean standardised uptake value (SUV mean), and maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max)

questions.question: Which of the following components is combined in the FDA-approved Prostate Health Index (PHI) test?

options.text: Free PSA, total PSA, and [-2]pro-PSA isoform (p2PSA)
Total PSA, intact PSA, and kallikrein-like peptidase 2
Free PSA, complexed PSA, and human kallikrein 2
Total PSA, percentage free PSA, and microseminoprotein-β

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.7.b states that the PHI test combines free and total PSA and the [-2]pro-PSA isoform (p2PSA), and this test is FDA-approved for reducing unnecessary prostate biopsies.
questions.fact: PHI test combines free and total PSA and the [-2]pro-PSA isoform (p2PSA)

questions.question: What fundamental technological difference distinguishes IsoPSA from the 4K score and PHI tests?

options.text: IsoPSA utilises a technology which focuses on the structure of PSA
IsoPSA analyzes circulating tumor DNA in plasma
IsoPSA uses a polygenic risk score algorithm

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.7.b explicitly states that 'In contrast to the 4K score and PHI, which focus on the concentration of PSA isoforms, IsoPSA utilises a technology which focuses on the structure of PSA.'
questions.fact: IsoPSA focuses on the structure of PSA, unlike 4K score and PHI which focus on concentration of PSA isoforms

questions.question: The Proclarix test has been correlated with detection of clinically significant prostate cancer notably in which specific clinical scenario?

options.text: Men with PSA levels above 20 ng/mL
Patients with equivocal MRI findings (PI-RADS 3 lesions)
Men with prior negative prostate biopsies
Patients with family history of prostate cancer

questions.explanation: Section 5.2.7.b states that 'Proclarix test is a blood-based test...This test has been correlated with the detection of csPCa, notably in case of equivocal MRI (PI-RADS 3 lesions).'
questions.fact: Proclarix test has been correlated with detection of csPCa notably in case of equivocal MRI (PI-RADS 3 lesions)

questions.question: According to the ASCO-EAU-AUA multidisciplinary expert panel recommendations, how many commercially available tissue-based prostate cancer biomarker tests met their criteria for inclusion?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that recommendations were limited to five commercially available tests: Oncotype Dx, Prolaris, Decipher, Decipher PORTOS and ProMark, which had extensive validation in large retrospective studies.
questions.section_title: 5.3.4. Tissue samples for homologous recombination repair (HRR) testing
questions.fact: The recommendations were limited to five commercially available tests (Oncotype Dx, Prolaris, Decipher, Decipher PORTOS and ProMark)

questions.question: In which clinical scenario should tissue biomarker testing be offered according to the guideline?

options.text: Routinely for all prostate cancer patients to guide initial diagnosis
Only in subsets of patients where the test result provides clinically actionable information
Mandatory for all patients with high-risk prostate cancer before treatment decisions
Only after genomic sequencing has confirmed HRR gene mutations

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.4 explicitly states that the Panel concluded these tests should not be offered routinely, but only in subsets of patients for which the test result provides clinically actionable information, such as men with favourable intermediate-risk PCa who might opt for AS.
questions.section_title: 5.3.4. Tissue samples for homologous recombination repair (HRR) testing
questions.fact: The Panel concluded that these tests should not be offered routinely, but only in subsets of patients for which the test result provides clinically actionable information

questions.question: What was the hazard ratio for distant metastasis associated with a high-risk Decipher test result in the RCT with 215 intermediate-risk prostate cancer patients?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.4 states that the RCT showed Decipher test indicating high risk proved to be prognostic for disease progression (HR 1.12), biochemical failure (HR 1.22), distant metastasis (HR 1.28) and PCSM (HR 1.45).
questions.section_title: 5.3.4. Tissue samples for homologous recombination repair (HRR) testing
questions.fact: Distant metastasis (HR 1.28)

questions.question: According to the recommended item list for tissue-based prognostic biomarker testing, which of the following should be documented for carcinoma-negative MRI-targeted biopsy?

options.text: Benign prostatic hyperplasia or stromal fibrosis

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states in section 5.3.3 that for carcinoma-negative MRI-targeted biopsy, specific benign pathology such as fibromuscular hyperplasia or granulomatous inflammation should be documented if present.
questions.fact: For carcinoma-negative MRI-targeted biopsy, specific benign pathology, e.g. fibromuscular hyperplasia or granulomatous inflammation

questions.question: Which specific element should be reported for Gleason Score 7 (GS 7) biopsies according to the recommended item list?

options.text: Percentage of Gleason grade 4 in GS 7 biopsies
Total percentage of Grade 4 and Grade 5 combined
Absolute length of Grade 4 pattern in millimeters
Number of cores containing Grade 4 pattern

questions.explanation: The guideline section 5.3.3 explicitly includes 'Percentage of Gleason grade 4 in Gleason Score (GS) 7 biopsies' as a recommended item to be reported.
questions.fact: Percentage of Gleason grade 4 in Gleason Score (GS) 7 biopsies

questions.question: When assessing intraductal/invasive cribriform carcinoma, what additional finding should be documented in cribriform-negative cases?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states in section 5.3.3 that 'Presence of Atypical Intraductal Proliferation (AIP)' should be documented in intraductal/invasive cribriform-negative cases.
questions.fact: Presence of Atypical Intraductal Proliferation (AIP) in intraductal/invasive cribriform-negative cases

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the maximum number of prostate biopsy cores that can be embedded per tissue cassette when individual cores can be clearly identified?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.2 states that if individual cores can clearly be identified in submitted jars, a maximum of three cores should be embedded per tissue cassette to ensure proper processing and optimal results.
questions.fact: If individual cores can clearly be identified in submitted jars, a maximum of three cores should be embedded per tissue cassette

questions.question: What is the purpose of cutting paraffin blocks at three levels as recommended in the guideline?

options.text: To reduce laboratory processing time and costs
To optimise detection of small lesions and improve accuracy of grading
To eliminate the need for immunohistochemistry staining
To ensure proper fixation of the tissue samples

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.2 explicitly states that to optimise detection of small lesions and improve accuracy of grading, paraffin blocks should be cut at three levels, with intervening unstained sections kept for potential immunohistochemistry.
questions.fact: To optimise detection of small lesions and improve accuracy of grading, paraffin blocks should be cut at three levels

questions.question: According to the guideline, which factor has been shown to significantly correlate with PCa detection rate in prostate biopsy processing?

options.text: The number of biopsy sites sampled
The patient's age at time of biopsy
The time between biopsy and processing

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.2 clearly states that the length of biopsy tissue significantly correlates with the PCa detection rate, highlighting the importance of obtaining adequate tissue lengths during biopsy procedures.
questions.fact: The length of biopsy tissue significantly correlates with the PCa detection rate

questions.question: At what age should men with BRCA2 mutations begin early PSA testing for prostate cancer according to the EAU guidelines?
questions.explanation: According to Section 5.3, men carrying BRCA2 mutations should be offered early PSA testing from 40 years of age. This is the most aggressive age threshold among the risk groups, reflecting the higher genetic risk associated with BRCA2 mutations.
questions.fact: males carrying breast cancer gene 2 (BRCA2) mutations from 40 years of age

questions.question: For asymptomatic males with a PSA level between 3-10 ng/mL, what is the recommended next step before further investigations?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3 states that asymptomatic males with a PSA level between 3 and 10 ng/mL should have repeat PSA testing prior to further investigations (Weak recommendation). This applies to the lower range of the PSA threshold.
questions.fact: In asymptomatic males with a PSA level between 3 and 10 ng/mL initially repeat PSA testing prior to further investigations

questions.question: What is the minimum life expectancy required for men to potentially benefit from early prostate cancer detection?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3 clearly states that males with a life expectancy of less than 15 years are unlikely to benefit from early diagnosis of PCa. An individualised risk-adapted strategy should be offered to well-informed males with a life expectancy of at least 15 years.
questions.fact: Males who have a life expectancy of less than 15 years are unlikely to benefit

questions.question: For early PSA testing, what PSA threshold at age 40 triggers a two-year follow-up interval according to the risk-adapted strategy?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3 specifies that males with a PSA level greater than 1 ng/mL at 40 years of age should receive a risk-adapted strategy with two-year follow-up intervals. This is one of the two age-specific thresholds for initiating regular monitoring.
questions.fact: males with a PSA level of > 1 ng/mL at 40 years of age; males with a PSA level of > 2ng/mL at 60 years of age

questions.question: Which of the following has a STRONG recommendation rating for biopsy indication in asymptomatic males with PSA between 3-20 ng/mL?

options.text: Additional serum or urine biomarker test
Magnetic resonance imaging of the prostate

questions.explanation: Section 5.3 assigns a Strong recommendation to MRI of the prostate for biopsy indication in asymptomatic males with PSA between 3-20 ng/mL. Risk calculators and biomarker tests receive Weak recommendations in this context.
questions.fact: magnetic resonance imaging of the prostate. Strong risk calculator, provided it is correctly calibrated to the population prevalence. an additional serum or urine biomarker test. Weak

questions.question: Germline testing for BRCA mutations on somatic testing carries what recommendation strength?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3 explicitly states 'Offer germline testing to patients with BRCA mutations on somatic testing' as a Strong recommendation. This is distinct from the Weak recommendation for family history criteria.
questions.fact: Offer germline testing to patients with BRCA mutations on somatic testing. Strong

questions.question: What type of reporting is recommended for radical prostatectomy specimens to ensure transparent and complete pathology reporting?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that 'the use of synoptic/synoptic-like or checklist reporting is recommended' for RP specimens, noting that 'Synoptic reporting results in more transparent and complete pathology reporting' (Section 5.3.5.c).
questions.fact: Synoptic/synoptic-like or checklist reporting is recommended for RP specimens as it results in more transparent and complete pathology reporting.

questions.question: When carcinoma is present at the surgical margin in a prostatectomy specimen, which of the following does Table 5.3 NOT specifically require to be documented?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that when carcinoma is at the margin, documentation must include sites, extent (focal or extensive), and highest Gleason grade at margin. Pre-operative PSA velocity is not mentioned in the margin reporting requirements (Section 5.3.5.c).
questions.fact: Surgical margin documentation must include sites, extent (focal or extensive), and highest Gleason grade at margin when carcinoma is present at the margin.

questions.question: What was the success rate of sequencing for homologous recombination repair (HRR) testing in the PROfound trial?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5 states that HRR testing in the PROfound trial was conducted with successful sequencing in 69% of cases. The 81% and 92% figures refer to ctDNA positive and negative percentage agreement, respectively.
questions.fact: Homologous recombination repair testing in the PROfound trial was conducted on archival or recent biopsy tissue from primary or metastatic disease with successful sequencing in 69%

questions.question: Which type of biopsy might be associated with lower success rates for HRR testing due to decalcification?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5 explicitly states that bone biopsies might be associated with lower success rates related to decalcification of tissue. The other biopsy types are not mentioned in relation to lower success rates in the text.
questions.fact: Bone biopsies might be associated with lower success rates related to decalcification of tissue

questions.question: When tumour tissue is not available for HRR testing, what might be a good alternative according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5 states that testing of circulating tumour DNA might be a good alternative if tumour tissue is not available. The other options (MRI-guided biopsy, PET-CT imaging, urinary biomarkers) are not mentioned as alternatives in this context.
questions.fact: Testing of circulating tumour DNA might be a good alternative if tumour tissue is not available

questions.question: What was the negative percentage agreement of circulating tumour DNA (ctDNA) testing when validated against tissue testing?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5 states that with tissue as reference, ctDNA showed 81% positive and 92% negative percentage agreement. The 81% figure refers to positive agreement, not negative.
questions.fact: With tissue as reference, ctDNA showed 81% positive and 92% negative percentage agreement

questions.question: Which histological grading system is described as the strongest prognostic factor for clinical behaviour and treatment response in conventional prostatic adenocarcinoma?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.d explicitly states that 'Grading of conventional prostatic adenocarcinoma using the Gleason system is the strongest prognostic factor for clinical behaviour and treatment response.' The Gleason system is specifically identified as the strongest prognostic factor among histological grading systems.
questions.fact: Grading of conventional prostatic adenocarcinoma using the Gleason system is the strongest prognostic factor for clinical behaviour and treatment response.

questions.question: In prostatectomy specimens, what percentage threshold is used to define minor high-grade components that are excluded from the ISUP GG?
questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.d states that the minor exception in prostatectomy specimens involves 'the exclusion of minor (<5%) high-grade components from the ISUP GG.' This 5% threshold is explicitly defined in the guideline as the cutoff for what constitutes a minor component.
questions.fact: Minor (<5%) high-grade components are excluded from the ISUP GG in prostatectomy specimens.

questions.question: In cases of multifocal prostate carcinoma, which tumour is selected for determining the ISUP GG?

options.text: The tumour with the highest grade, stage, or volume (index lesion)
The most accessible tumour on biopsy

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.d explicitly states that 'In case of multifocality, the ISUP GG of the index lesion (i.e. the tumour having the highest grade, stage or volume) is specified.' The index lesion is defined by the highest grade, stage, OR volume, not solely by size.
questions.fact: In case of multifocality, the ISUP GG of the index lesion (i.e. the tumour having the highest grade, stage or volume) is specified.

questions.question: Which of the following has been shown to correlate with prostate cancer (PCa) volume at radical prostatectomy (RP)?

options.text: Tumour grade, stage, and surgical margin status
Patient age and body mass index
Lymph node involvement and extracapsular extension

questions.explanation: According to section 5.3.5.f, PCa volume at RP has been shown to correlate with tumour grade, stage, and surgical margin status. The independent prognostic value of PCa volume itself has not been established.
questions.fact: Although PCa volume at RP correlates with tumour grade, stage and surgical margin status, the independent prognostic value of PCa volume has not been established

questions.question: What is the status of the independent prognostic value of pathological tumour volume at radical prostatectomy?

options.text: It has been established and is routinely used
It is currently under investigation in clinical trials
It is significant only in high-grade tumours

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.f explicitly states that the independent prognostic value of PCa volume at RP has not been established. This is why reporting of tumour volume details remains optional.
questions.fact: Since the independent value of pathological tumour volume at RP has not been established, reporting of the diameter/volume of the dominant tumour nodule, or a rough estimate of the percentage of cancer tissue, is optional

questions.question: According to the guideline, which reporting element is considered optional at radical prostatectomy?

options.text: Gleason score and ISUP grade group
Diameter/volume of the dominant tumour nodule or percentage of cancer tissue

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.f specifies that since the independent prognostic value of pathological tumour volume has not been established, reporting of the diameter/volume of the dominant tumour nodule or a rough estimate of the percentage of cancer tissue is optional. Core parameters like Gleason score and TNM staging are mandatory.
questions.fact: Since the independent value of pathological tumour volume at RP has not been established, reporting of the diameter/volume of the dominant tumour nodule, or a rough estimate of the percentage of cancer tissue, is optional

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the definition of extraprostatic extension (EPE) in prostate cancer?

options.text: Carcinoma confined to the peripheral zone of the prostate
Carcinoma mixed with periprostatic adipose tissue, or tissue extending beyond prostate gland boundaries
Carcinoma involving more than 50% of the prostate volume

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that EPE is defined as carcinoma mixed with periprostatic adipose tissue, or tissue that extends beyond the prostate gland boundaries (e.g., neurovascular bundle, anterior prostate). Section 5.3.5.e.
questions.fact: Extraprostatic extension is defined as carcinoma mixed with periprostatic adipose tissue, or tissue that extends beyond the prostate gland boundaries (e.g., neurovascular bundle, anterior prostate).

questions.question: What stage is assigned when tumour invades the bladder muscle wall macroscopically?
questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that stage pT4 is assigned when the tumour invades the bladder muscle wall as determined macroscopically. Section 5.3.5.e.
questions.fact: Stage pT4 is assigned when the tumour invades the bladder muscle wall as determined macroscopically.

questions.question: According to the systematic review and meta-analysis cited in the guideline, what is the relationship between focal and extensive extraprostatic extension regarding biochemical recurrence-free survival?

options.text: Extensive EPE shows significantly better BCR-free survival than focal EPE
Extensive EPE shows significantly worse BCR-free survival than focal EPE
There is no statistically significant difference between focal and extensive EPE for BCR-free survival
Focal EPE always progresses to extensive EPE within 5 years

questions.explanation: The guideline cites a systematic review and meta-analysis that found no statistically significant difference between focal and extensive EPE for BCR-free survival, despite some individual studies suggesting an association with recurrence risk. Section 5.3.5.e.
questions.fact: A systematic review and meta-analysis did not find a statistically significant difference between focal and extensive EPE for BCR-free survival.

questions.question: At which anatomical location does tumour mixed with skeletal muscle NOT constitute extraprostatic extension?

options.text: At the apex of the prostate

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that at the apex of the prostate, tumour mixed with skeletal muscle does not constitute EPE. This is a specific exception to the definition of EPE. Section 5.3.5.e.
questions.fact: At the apex of the prostate, tumour mixed with skeletal muscle does not constitute EPE.

questions.question: According to the guideline, microscopic invasion of smooth muscle fibres at the bladder neck should be classified as:
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that microscopic invasion of smooth muscle fibres at the bladder neck is not equated to bladder wall invasion (not pT4) because it does not carry independent prognostic significance, and should be recorded as EPE (pT3a). Section 5.3.5.e.
questions.fact: Microscopic invasion of smooth muscle fibres is not equated to bladder wall invasion, i.e. not as pT4, because it does not carry independent prognostic significance for PCa recurrence and should be recorded as EPE (pT3a).

questions.question: According to the guideline, what are the two most common types of prostate cancer?

options.text: Small cell and large cell carcinoma
Basal cell and adenoid cystic carcinoma

questions.explanation: Section 5.4 explicitly states that 'acinar and ductal adenocarcinoma are, by far, the most common types of PCa'. The other options describe rare aggressive subtypes that are not the most common types.
questions.fact: Acinar and ductal adenocarcinoma are, by far, the most common types of PCa

questions.question: In which clinical setting are neuroendocrine transformation, (adeno)squamous, sarcomatoid, and pleomorphic giant cell carcinoma predominantly found?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4 clearly states that these rare aggressive PCa types are 'predominantly found in the CRPC setting, but can also occur at primary diagnosis'. The key distinction is their primary association with CRPC.
questions.fact: These (sub)types of PCa are predominantly found in the CRPC setting, but can also occur at primary diagnosis

questions.question: What type of therapy do neuroendocrine, sarcomatoid, and pleomorphic giant cell carcinoma subtypes of prostate cancer have limited sensitivity to?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4 explicitly states that these respective subtypes 'have limited sensitivity to androgen-deprivation therapies'. This is a critical clinical consideration for treatment planning.
questions.fact: Respective subtypes together with adenoid cystic (basal cell) carcinoma have limited sensitivity to androgen-deprivation therapies

questions.question: Which of the following is a synonym for adenocarcinoma with diffuse neuroendocrine differentiation?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4 lists 'amphicrine (synonym: adenocarcinoma with diffuse neuroendocrine differentiation)' as one of the neuroendocrine transformation types. The other options are distinct rare subtypes, not synonyms for each other.
questions.fact: Neuroendocrine transformation can occur as small cell, large cell or amphicrine (synonym: adenocarcinoma with diffuse neuroendocrine differentiation) carcinoma

questions.question: According to the guideline, surgical margin status is considered positive under which of the following conditions?

options.text: Tumour cells are in contact with the ink on the specimen surface
Tumour cells are within 3 mm of the inked surface
Tumour cells are at the surface of tissue lacking ink
Tumour cells are identified in multiple tissue blocks

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.g states that margin status is positive if tumour cells are in contact with the ink on the specimen surface. Option B describes a distance threshold not mentioned in the text, Option C describes negative margin status, and Option D is not related to margin positivity criteria.
questions.fact: Margin status is positive if tumour cells are in contact with the ink on the specimen surface.

questions.question: What is the recommended classification system for the extent of surgical margin positivity according to the guideline?

options.text: Single block vs. multiple blocks affected

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.g explicitly states that extent of margin positivity should be classified as focal, ≤1 mm vs. extensive, >1 mm. The 3 mm threshold mentioned in Option B applies to dichotomised measurements for BCR-free survival analysis, not for the extent classification.
questions.fact: The linear extent in millimetres of involvement: focal, ≤1 mm vs. extensive, >1 mm.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the relationship between surgical margin status and extraprostatic extension (EPE) is correct?

options.text: A positive margin is considered evidence of EPE
Surgical margin status is part of pathological staging
Surgical margin is separate from pathological stage
EPE must be present for a margin to be positive

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.g clearly states that surgical margin is separate from pathological stage, and a positive margin is not evidence of EPE. Option A contradicts this statement, Option B incorrectly implies margin status is part of staging, and Option D is not supported by the text.
questions.fact: Surgical margin is separate from pathological stage, and a positive margin is not evidence of EPE.

questions.question: In which clinical scenario may it not be possible to determine surgical margin status?

options.text: When tumour cells are identified close to the inked surface
In tissues with severe crush artefacts
When the Gleason score is less than 7
In cases of low-grade prostate cancer

questions.explanation: Section 5.3.5.g explicitly states that in tissues that have severe crush artefacts, it may not be possible to determine margin status. Options A, C, and D describe conditions that do not affect the ability to assess margin status according to the text.
questions.fact: In tissues that have severe crush artefacts, it may not be possible to determine margin status.

questions.question: In the classic diagnostic algorithm for prostate cancer, what was the indication for biopsy generally solely based on?

options.text: PSA threshold or abnormal digital rectal examination (DRE)
PSA threshold or abnormal transrectal ultrasound findings
MRI PI-RADS score or abnormal digital rectal examination (DRE)
Serum biomarker panel or imaging findings

questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states in Section 5.4.1.a that 'in the classic diagnostic algorithm the indication for biopsy was generally solely based on a PSA threshold or abnormal DRE'. This represents the historical approach before the introduction of more sophisticated diagnostic pathways.
questions.section_title: 5.4.1.a. Risk calculators assessing the risk of csPCa
questions.fact: In the classic diagnostic algorithm the indication for biopsy was generally solely based on a PSA threshold or abnormal DRE

questions.question: According to the guideline, elevated risk of significant prostate cancer is established based on which of the following primary diagnostic tools?

options.text: PSA level, serum biomarkers, DRE, and primary imaging
Prostate-specific antigen density and family history only

questions.explanation: The guideline text in Section 5.4.1.a clearly states that 'An elevated risk of significant PCa is established based on one or more of the primary diagnostic tools applied, such as PSA level, other serum biomarker, DRE or primary imaging'. The other options describe tools not mentioned as primary diagnostic tools in this context.
questions.section_title: 5.4.1.a. Risk calculators assessing the risk of csPCa
questions.fact: An elevated risk of significant PCa is established based on one or more of the primary diagnostic tools applied, such as PSA level, other serum biomarker, DRE or primary imaging

questions.question: The chosen diagnostic algorithm for prostate cancer evaluation can be elected based on all of the following EXCEPT:

options.text: Prevalence of disease in men entering the pathway
Different sequences and combinations of diagnostic tools

questions.explanation: Section 5.4.1.a states the algorithm 'can be elected based on availability, expertise and resources' and 'on prevalence of disease in men entering the pathway (e.g. screening versus clinical symptoms)'. Geographic residence is not mentioned as a decision factor in the text.
questions.section_title: 5.4.1.a. Risk calculators assessing the risk of csPCa
questions.fact: The chosen diagnostic algorithm can be elected based on availability, expertise and resources

questions.question: Which risk calculator was updated by incorporating the 2014 ISUP Pathology Gleason Grading and Cribriform growth?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4.1.b states that the calculator derived from the ERSPC cohort was updated by incorporating the 2014 ISUP Pathology Gleason Grading and Cribriform growth.
questions.section_title: 5.4.1.b. Using risk stratification to avoid magnetic resonance imaging scans and biopsy procedures
questions.fact: The calculator derived from the ERSPC cohort was updated by incorporating the 2014 ISUP Pathology Gleason Grading and Cribriform growth [181].

questions.question: What is the main limitation of risk calculators as stated in the guideline?

options.text: They require invasive procedures to function
They are only available in select countries
They depend on disease prevalence in the population
They have been shown to be inaccurate in all settings

questions.explanation: Section 5.4.1.b explicitly states that calculators are limited by their dependency on disease prevalence, and all calculators show miscalibration when tested in populations with a different prevalence than the training population.
questions.section_title: 5.4.1.b. Using risk stratification to avoid magnetic resonance imaging scans and biopsy procedures
questions.fact: Calculators are limited by their dependency on disease prevalence. All calculators show miscalibration when tested in populations with a different prevalence than that of the training population of the model.

questions.question: According to Table 5.4, which risk calculator has the highest aggregated AUC (area under curve) value?

options.text: Van Leeuwen calculator with AUC of 0.86

questions.explanation: Table 5.4 in section 5.4.1.b lists the Van Leeuwen calculator as having an AUC of 0.86, which is the highest among all the listed calculators, including those validated before and after MRI.
questions.section_title: 5.4.1.b. Using risk stratification to avoid magnetic resonance imaging scans and biopsy procedures
questions.fact: Table 5.4 lists Van Leeuwen calculator with AUC (aggregated) of 0.86, which is the highest AUC value among all available risk calculators listed.

questions.question: Which of the following risk calculators includes PI-RADS as one of its factors?
questions.explanation: Table 5.4 in section 5.4.1.b shows that Van Leeuwen calculator and MRI-RPCRC-RC3/4 include PI-RADS as factors, while the other listed calculators do not include PI-RADS in their factors.
questions.section_title: 5.4.1.b. Using risk stratification to avoid magnetic resonance imaging scans and biopsy procedures
questions.fact: Table 5.4 shows that Van Leeuwen (PI-RADS included) and MRI-RPCRC-RC3/4 (PI-RADS included) are the calculators that incorporate PI-RADS scoring.

questions.question: According to the retrospective analysis of 200 men from a prospective database who underwent MRI and biopsy, what percentage of men could have avoided MRI and biopsy using the Rotterdam Prostate Cancer Risk Calculator upfront?
questions.explanation: The text states 'A retrospective analysis including 200 men from a prospective database... showed that upfront use of the Rotterdam Prostate Cancer Risk Calculator would have avoided MRI and biopsy in 73 men (37%).' This 37% figure is directly stated in the guideline section.
questions.fact: A retrospective analysis of 200 men showed that upfront use of the Rotterdam Prostate Cancer Risk Calculator would have avoided MRI and biopsy in 37% (73 men)

questions.question: In the prospective multicentre trial including 532 men, what threshold for the Stockholm3 test was evaluated to determine MRI and biopsy eligibility, and what percentage of procedures could have been avoided?

options.text: Stockholm3 test threshold ≥10%, avoiding 38% of procedures
Stockholm3 test threshold ≥15%, avoiding 30% of procedures

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'Another prospective multicentre trial including 532 men... showed that using a threshold of ≥10% for the Stockholm3 test to perform MRI and biopsy would have avoided MRI and biopsy in 38% of men.' The correct answer combines both the threshold value and the percentage avoided.
questions.fact: Using a threshold of ≥10% for the Stockholm3 test would have avoided MRI and biopsy in 38% of men

questions.question: According to the guideline, what follow-up condition is recommended for patients with a low risk score on risk calculators to minimize potential harm from missed cancers?

options.text: Offer immediate repeat biopsy within 3 months
Offer repeat testing and follow-up until life expectancy <15 years
Recommend active surveillance regardless of cancer grade

questions.explanation: The conclusion section states 'as long as patients with a low risk score on the risk calculator are offered repeat testing and follow-up until they have a life expectancy of <15 years, it seems unlikely that any preliminary missed case would cause increased morbidity or lead to PCSM.' This specific condition is directly stated in the text.
questions.fact: Patients with low risk scores should be offered repeat testing and follow-up until they have a life expectancy of <15 years

questions.question: According to the meta-analysis on PI-RADS v2.1 data, what are the sensitivity and specificity values for PI-RADS ≥3 threshold in detecting ISUP GG ≥2 cancer on a patient level?
questions.explanation: The meta-analysis on PI-RADS v2.1 data shows that PI-RADS ≥3 thresholding demonstrated sensitivity of 96% and specificity of 43% for ISUP GG ≥2 cancer on a patient level, based on 15 reports including 4,484 men.
questions.fact: PI-RADS ≥3 thresholding showed MRI sensitivity/specificity for significant disease of 96%/43% on a patient level for ISUP GG ≥2 cancer (15 reports, 4,484 men)

questions.question: In the MR PROPER trial comparing MRI pathway versus systematic biopsy based on risk calculator, what percentage of biopsies were avoided by the MRI pathway?
questions.explanation: The MR PROPER trial demonstrated that the MRI pathway avoided biopsy in 55% of men (559/1015) compared to 42% (403/950) avoided by the risk calculator strategy (difference -13%, 95% CI: -17% to -8.3%; p < 0.01).
questions.fact: The MRI pathway avoided biopsy in more men as compared to the diagnostic pathway using a risk calculator (559/1015, 55% vs. 403/950, 42%)

questions.question: What percentage of ISUP GG ≥2 cancers would be missed if using a PI-RADS ≥3 threshold to avoid biopsies, according to pooled studies?
questions.explanation: In pooled studies on biopsy-naïve patients and patients with prior negative biopsies, a Likert/PI-RADS threshold of ≥3 would have missed 11% (95% CI: 6-18) of all detected ISUP GG ≥2 cancers while avoiding 30% of all biopsy procedures.
questions.fact: A Likert/PI-RADS threshold of ≥3 would have avoided 30% (95% CI: 23-38) of all biopsy procedures while missing 11% (95% CI: 6-18) of all detected ISUP GG ≥2 cancers

questions.question: According to the guideline, what are the two approaches for prostate biopsy mentioned?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.1 explicitly states that prostate biopsy can be performed using various strategies and approaches, specifically mentioning 'transperineal versus transrectal' as the two approaches.
questions.fact: The two approaches mentioned are transperineal and transrectal.

questions.question: What type of clinical data do comprehensive MRI-based risk calculators combine to predict biopsy results?
questions.explanation: According to Section 5.4.2.d, several groups have developed comprehensive risk calculators that combine MRI findings with simple clinical data as a tool to predict subsequent biopsy results.
questions.fact: Several groups have developed comprehensive risk calculators that combine MRI findings with simple clinical data as a tool to predict subsequent biopsy results

questions.question: Which aspects of MRI-based risk calculators showed good results during external validation?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4.2.d states that some calculators underwent external validation with good results both in terms of discrimination and clinical utility.
questions.fact: Some calculators underwent external validation with good results both in terms of discrimination and clinical utility

questions.question: What factor hinders the use of MRI-based risk calculators in population screening?
questions.explanation: Section 5.4.2.d explicitly states that the use of MRI-based risk calculators is hindered by their miscalibration due to prevalence dependency.
questions.fact: However, their use is hindered by their miscalibration due to prevalence dependency

questions.question: In the OPTIMUM trial, what was the randomization ratio for patients assigned to micro-US, micro-US/MRI, and MRI/conUS biopsy groups?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5 states that patients were randomised in a 1:2:3 ratio to undergo micro-US-guided biopsy, combined MRI/micro-US fusion-guided biopsy (micro-US/MRI), or software-assisted MRI/conventional US fusion-guided biopsy (MRI/conUS).
questions.fact: Patients were randomised in a 1:2:3 ratio to undergo micro-US-guided biopsy without MRI (micro-US), combined MRI/micro-US fusion-guided biopsy (micro-US/MRI), or software-assisted MRI/conventional US fusion-guided biopsy (MRI/conUS).

questions.question: What did micro-US-guided biopsy demonstrate compared to MRI fusion-guided biopsy in the OPTIMUM trial?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5 reports that micro-US-guided biopsy was non-inferior to MRI fusion-guided biopsy with a difference of 3.5% (95% CI: -3.95% to 10.92%) and non-inferiority p-value <0.001, meeting the pre-specified non-inferiority margin of 10%.
questions.fact: Micro-US-guided biopsy was non-inferior to MRI fusion-guided biopsy (difference, 3.5% [95% CI: −3.95% - 10.92%]; non-inferiority p < .001; non-inferiority margin set at 10%).

questions.question: According to the OPTIMUM trial, what percentage of patients who underwent micro-US had negative findings (PI-RIMUS 1-2) compared to those who underwent MRI (PI-RADS 1-2)?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5 notes that 20% of patients who had micro-US had negative findings (PI-RIMUS 1-2), while 32% of patients who underwent MRI had negative findings (PI-RADS 1-2), suggesting the micro-US pathway could avoid more biopsies than the MRI pathway.
questions.fact: Of note, 20% of the patients who had micro-US had negative findings (PIRIMUS 1-2), while 32% of patients who underwent MRI had negative findings (PI-RADS 1-2) suggesting that the micro-US pathway could avoid less biopsies than the MRI pathway.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a method for obtaining MR-targeted biopsy when MRI has shown a suspicious lesion?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.3 explicitly states that MR-targeted biopsy can be obtained through cognitive guidance, US/MR fusion software, or direct in-bore guidance. Transrectal ultrasound only guidance is not listed as a method for MR-targeted biopsy.
questions.fact: MR-targeted biopsy can be obtained through cognitive guidance, US/MR fusion software or direct in-bore guidance

questions.question: According to current literature including systematic reviews and meta-analyses, what is the relationship between the three image-guided MRI-targeted biopsy techniques?

options.text: US/MR fusion software is clearly superior
Direct in-bore guidance is clearly superior
No clear superiority of one technique over another has been demonstrated

questions.explanation: Section 5.5.3 states that 'Current literature, including SRs and meta-analyses, does not show a clear superiority of one image-guided technique over another [407-409].'
questions.fact: Current literature, including SRs and meta-analyses, does not show a clear superiority of one image-guided technique over another

questions.question: The FUTURE randomised trial compared three MRI-targeted biopsy techniques and found no differences in cancer detection. What limitation of this trial was noted?

options.text: It only included patients with initial biopsy
It was overpowered for its conclusion
It was underpowered for its conclusion
It only compared two techniques instead of three

questions.explanation: Section 5.5.3 mentions that the FUTURE trial 'found no differences in cancer detection [408]. However, it was underpowered for this conclusion.' The text explicitly notes this limitation regarding the trial's statistical power.
questions.fact: The FUTURE trial was underpowered for its conclusion

questions.question: According to the 2006 systematic review cited in the guidelines, what is the minimum number of cores required for systematic biopsies to achieve optimal cancer detection?
questions.explanation: Section 5.5.2 cites a 2006 systematic review showing that 12 is the minimum number of cores for systematic biopsies, with >12 cores not increasing cancer detection rate significantly.
questions.fact: A 2006 SR showed that 12 is the minimum number of cores for systematic biopsies, with >12 cores not increasing cancer detection rate significantly.

questions.question: In systematic biopsies where no prior imaging is used for targeting, where should the sample sites be located?

options.text: Unilateral from apex to base only
Bilateral from apex to base, as far posterior and lateral as possible in the peripheral gland
Primarily in the transition zone of the prostate
Concentrated in the central gland near the urethra

questions.explanation: Section 5.5.2 explicitly states that for systematic biopsies without imaging guidance, sample sites should be bilateral from apex to base, as far posterior and lateral as possible in the peripheral gland.
questions.fact: For systematic biopsies in which no prior imaging is used for targeting, the sample sites should be bilateral from apex to base, as far posterior and lateral as possible in the peripheral gland.

questions.question: What happens to cancer detection rate when more than 12 systematic biopsy cores are taken?

options.text: Detection rate remains stable regardless of core number
Detection rate decreases due to sample quality degradation
Cancer detection rate is not increased significantly

questions.explanation: Section 5.5.2 states that the 2006 systematic review demonstrated that >12 cores do not increase cancer detection rate significantly, establishing 12 as the optimal minimum.
questions.fact: A 2006 SR showed that 12 is the minimum number of cores for systematic biopsies, with >12 cores not increasing cancer detection rate significantly.

questions.question: In the PRECISION and PRECISE trials, what was the maximum number of cores taken during MRI-targeted biopsy in cases of positive MRI?
questions.explanation: The PRECISION and PRECISE trials randomised patients to either ten- to twelve-core systematic biopsy or to MRI with subsequent MRI-targeted biopsy (up to four cores) in case of positive MRI. This demonstrates that MRI-targeted biopsy uses significantly fewer cores than systematic biopsy.
questions.section_title: 5.5.3.b. Reduced detection of cancers labelled ISUP GG 1
questions.fact: MRI with subsequent MRI-targeted biopsy (up to four cores) in case of positive MRI

questions.question: What is the minimum number of cores required for proper sampling of an MRI-detected lesion?

questions.question: In patients with a unilateral MRI-detected lesion, what is the added value of contralateral systematic biopsy for diagnosing clinically significant prostate cancer?

questions.question: According to the meta-analysis cited in section 5.5.5, MRI-directed targeted and regional biopsy approach detected significantly more ISUP GG ≥ 2 cancers than MRI-targeted biopsy alone with which relative risk?

questions.question: In the German PROBASE trial post-hoc analysis, which biopsy method detected the highest proportion of Grade Group ≥2 cancers?

questions.question: In men aged 45-55 years in the PROBASE trial, what proportion of MRI examinations showed PI-RADS 3 findings?

questions.question: What is the strength of the recommendation against using MRI as an initial screening tool for prostate cancer?

questions.question: According to the guidelines, what PSA density threshold allows omitting biopsy when MRI is negative (PI-RADS ≤ 2) and clinical suspicion is low?
questions.explanation: Section 5.6 specifies that when MRI is negative (PI-RADS ≤ 2) and clinical suspicion of PCa is low, with PSA density < 0.20 ng/mL/cc and no family history, biopsy can be omitted and PSA monitoring offered instead.
questions.fact: Omit biopsy and offer PSA monitoring when MRI is negative (i.e. PI-RADS ≤ 2), and clinical suspicion of PCa is low (PSA density < 0.20 ng/mL/cc and no family history).

questions.question: In which PI-RADS category is targeted biopsy with perilesional sampling recommended?
questions.explanation: Section 5.6 states that targeted biopsy should be combined with perilesional sampling when MRI is positive, specifically when the lesion is classified as PI-RADS ≥ 4, indicating clinically significant findings warranting tissue diagnosis.
questions.fact: Combine targeted biopsy with perilesional sampling when MRI is positive (i.e. PI-RADS ≥ 4).

questions.question: When MRI is indeterminate (PI-RADS = 3), what additional PSA density threshold must be met to consider omitting biopsy?
questions.explanation: Section 5.6 specifies that for an indeterminate MRI (PI-RADS = 3), biopsy may be omitted only when clinical suspicion is very low, which is defined as PSA density < 0.10 ng/mL/cc and no family history. This is a stricter threshold than for PI-RADS ≤ 2.
questions.fact: When MRI is indeterminate (PI-RADS = 3), and clinical suspicion of PCa is very low (PSA density < 0.10 ng/mL/cc and no family history), omit biopsy and offer PSA monitoring.

questions.question: Which of the following is an acceptable method for obtaining MR-targeted biopsy?

questions.question: According to the systematic review and meta-analysis of 8 studies comparing MRI-targeted biopsies, what sensitivity for detection of clinically significant prostate cancer (csPCa) was reported for the transperineal approach?

questions.question: In the TRANSLATE RCT with 1,126 men randomised to compare MRI-guided transperineal and transrectal biopsies, what was the csPCa detection rate in the transperineal arm?

questions.question: According to the PERFECT trial, which biopsy approach demonstrated higher significant cancer detection rates for anterior prostate tumors?
